ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated shishamo code. A0CJW (smelt) used as closest defensible anchor for this semi-dried baked preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for shishamo or semi-dried smelt fish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of baked shishamo is 3 whole fish, approximately 60g, commonly served as a side dish in Japanese meals.

nutrient_source.title: Shishamo, semi-dried, baked — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB Foodo provenance with confidence 0.95 for all per_100g nutrient fields (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary_fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol). Values preserved as supplied by HPB. Saturated_fat_g estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for capelin, cooked (FDC ID 174202) as the closest approved-source analogue; monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g similarly estimated from the same USDA entry. Sugar_g and trans_fat_g confirmed zero per USDA capelin data. These estimations are documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Baked semi-dried shishamo contains only 0.2g carbohydrate per 100g. Per established GI methodology (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), glycemic index testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of 10–15g per serving; foods with negligible carbohydrate content are classified as GI not applicable. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per 60g serving = effectively 0.0 given negligible carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 640mg per 100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit baked semi-dried shishamo to 1–2 small fish per occasion, avoid additional salt or soy sauce, and consider soaking the fish in water for 10–15 minutes before baking to partially reduce sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.2g/100g) with high protein (24.3g/100g) and omega-3 fatty acids
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baked semi-dried shishamo is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content and high protein, which together produce no meaningful glycaemic impact; however, the high cholesterol (300mg/100g) warrants limiting portion size to 2–3 fish per serving, particularly for those with cardiovascular comorbidities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol (300mg/100g) and high sodium (640mg/100g) offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume baked semi-dried shishamo in moderation — no more than 2–3 fish per serving — given the high dietary cholesterol and sodium content, while recognising that the omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) present in shishamo may confer cardioprotective benefits when consumed as part of a balanced, sodium-controlled diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (anti-inflammatory) and calcium (360mg/100g from edible bones) are beneficial, but high sodium (640mg/100g) may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Shishamo provides omega-3 fatty acids and bone-protective calcium beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients, but the high sodium content (640mg/100g) may exacerbate joint swelling and should be managed by limiting to 2–3 fish per serving and avoiding additional salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked semi-dried shishamo is a Japanese fish dish made from shishamo (Spirinchus lanceolatus), a small smelt-like fish prized in Japanese cuisine for its edible roe-filled belly. The fish is first partially dried through a salting and drying process that concentrates flavour and firms the flesh, then baked or grilled until the exterior turns golden-brown and slightly crisp while the interior remains moist and tender. The result is a fish with a savoury, lightly salty flavour, a firm yet slightly dry texture, and a rich umami depth from the drying process. Shishamo is commonly served whole — typically 3 fish per serving — as a side dish (okazu) in Japanese set meals, bento boxes, and izakaya menus. Nutritionally, baked semi-dried shishamo is a high-protein food (24.3g/100g) with moderate fat (7.8g/100g) including beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, and an exceptionally high calcium content (360mg/100g) derived from the edible bones consumed whole. The semi-drying and salting process results in elevated sodium (640mg/100g), making this food unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or sodium-restricted diets without strict portion control. Cholesterol is high at 300mg/100g. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.2g/100g, making it appropriate for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns and suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The fish is a good source of potassium (400mg/100g) and iron (1.7mg/100g). Clinically, the omega-3 content supports cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits, while the high sodium and cholesterol require moderation for at-risk individuals.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in baked shishamo?
Is baked shishamo suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in baked shishamo?
Can I eat shishamo if I have high cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and sugar estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy capelin cooked data as the closest approved-source analogue and documented in notes.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is 0.2g — not zero; no zero-macro justification required for carbohydrate. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed as negligible/zero per HPB source for this preparation. Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source and USDA FoodData Central capelin data; shishamo contains no added or natural sugars. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for this fish preparation per USDA FoodData Central capelin data. Saturated_fat_g (1.8g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.9g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for capelin, cooked (FDC ID 174202), as the closest approved-source analogue to shishamo; these values are not reported in the HPB source for this specific entry. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.2g/100g); glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per the low-carbohydrate GI rule. GI source cited as Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) to satisfy the approved GI source requirement. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB or USDA for this specific preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for semi-dried baked shishamo not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Rheumatoid arthritis health context from input preserved and moved to additional_conditions. The input listed rheumatoid_arthritis as suitable: false with contradictory reasoning; concern_level set to moderate reflecting the dual benefit/risk profile.
